title: Te Whakamarumaru o te Taiao: Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Science
culturalContext: Students will explore kaitiakitanga through the lens of Ngāti Kahungunu's relationship with Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe. This awa is named for the bellbird (kōtuku) that once flourished in its surrounding māhoe trees, and holds significance in local pūrākau about the taniwha Hinekorako who protects its waters. Students will connect this traditional knowledge with contemporary water quality science.
learningObjectives: Measure and analyze at least three water quality parameters (pH, turbidity, nitrate levels) using scientific equipment at designated sites along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Compare current water quality data with historical mātauranga Māori indicators of stream health (e.g., presence of tuna, kōura, and native fish species) through interviews with local kaumātua
Design and propose one evidence-based action that applies both scientific understanding and Māori principles of kaitiakitanga to improve the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Create a detailed scientific diagram labeling the components of the Mangakōtukutuku stream ecosystem and explaining how human activities impact its mauri

activities.title: He Hīkoi, He Awa, He Kōrero: Stream Walk and Story
activities.description: Students embark on a guided hīkoi along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with a local kaumātua. They listen to the pūrākau of Hinekorako and learn traditional names for key features (puna, pari, etc.). They make initial observations and sketches of the stream environment, noting flora and fauna.

activities.title: Ngā Inenga Pūtaiao: Scientific Measurement Station
activities.description: At three pre-determined sites (upstream, mid-stream near college, downstream), students work in small groups to collect water samples. Using LaMotte water testing kits, they measure and record pH, nitrate levels, turbidity, and temperature. They document their methods and results scientifically.

activities.title: Te Kōrero me ngā Kaumātua: Interviewing the Knowledge Holders
activities.description: Back in the classroom, students prepare questions for a panel of local kaumātua and a representative from Hawke's Bay Regional Council. They interview them about historical changes in the stream's health, traditional indicators of well-being (e.g., abundance of kōura), and compare this mātauranga with their collected scientific data.

activities.title: Te Tohu Mauri: Interpreting the Data and the Mauri
activities.description: Students analyze their collected data, creating graphs to visualize changes along the stream's length. They engage in a discussion using the 'Mauri Model' framework to assess the stream's health from both a scientific and cultural perspective, synthesizing their findings from all sources.

activities.title: He Mahere Kaitiaki: Designing an Action Plan
activities.description: Based on their analysis, groups design a specific, actionable kaitiakitanga project (e.g., planting day for riparian margin, designing educational signs for the community, a proposal for a sediment trap). They create a poster or digital presentation outlining the science behind their plan and how it honours the mauri of the awa.

resources: LaMotte Water Monitoring Kit (or equivalent) for student water testing
Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu (e.g., Aunty Hine Smith) and a representative from Hawke's Bay Regional Council - Water Quality Team
Digital access to the Land Air Water Aotearoa (LAWA) website and its historical data for the Mangakōtukutuku stream
The picture book 'The River' by Sally Sutton and illustrated by Brian Lovelock, and the resource 'Te Mauri o te Wai' from the Māori Biosecurity Network

tasks: Completed field data booklet with scientific measurements, observations, and interview notes
A synthesized analysis graph comparing their data with one historical cultural indicator
A group presentation of their 'Mahere Kaitiaki' (Action Plan) poster

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Investigating in Science - Students will carry out science investigations using a variety of approaches.
Nature of Science: Communicating in Science - Students will begin to use a range of scientific symbols, conventions, and vocabulary.
Living World: Ecology - Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat and how they respond to environmental changes, both natural and human-induced.
Planet Earth and Beyond: Interacting Systems - Investigate the water cycle and its effect on climate, landforms, and life.
